title: Chapter 35: The Death of the Count
content: "You."

The word fell from what remained of Alexander Vlad's mouth like a stone into a grave. His lips were split, charred, peeling back from teeth that had yellowed the way teeth do when they've been in a skull for four hundred years. The left side of his face was still Vlad — still the cheekbones, still the jawline that had graced the covers of Forbes and Time — but the right side was something excavated. Sunken eye socket. Desiccated skin stretched over ancient geometry. The thing beneath the beauty, finally visible.
His remaining eye fixed on Marcus Webb. It burned. Not metaphorically. The iris caught the flicker of the shattered display screens behind them and threw it back like a coal.
"After everything I gave you."

Marcus stood between Vlad and Maya. His hands were shaking. They had been shaking since 1523, though he'd learned to hide it around year eighty. Five centuries of trembling, buried under servitude. He could feel the cold of the NYSE floor through his shoes, the way it bit into his soles, the way everything cold had always reminded him of Vlad's touch.
"You gave me nothing." Marcus's voice cracked on the last word. He swallowed smoke — the air tasted of burned plastic and ozone, bitter on his tongue. "You took everything."
Vlad moved.

Five centuries of predation compressed into a single lunge — faster than human, faster than should be possible for something that damaged. His hand closed around Marcus's throat. The grip was iron and ice and every nightmare Marcus had ever had, all the cigarettes stubbed out on his skin, all the times Vlad had made him crawl, all the crickets.
But the grip was wrong.

Weak. Desperate. The fingers couldn't close properly — the UV had cooked the tendons in his right hand, and they flexed uselessly, slipping on Marcus's collar. Vlad's speed was there, but his strength was hemorrhaging out of him like light through broken glass.

Marcus didn't run. He stood in that failing grip and looked at the thing that had owned him. The charred half of Vlad's face twitched. The good eye was wild, darting — not the gaze of an apex predator. The gaze of something cornered. Something that had hidden behind perfection for so long that it no longer knew how to exist without the mask.

For five hundred years, Marcus had seen Vlad as a god. Terrible and beautiful and inevitable. The way the moon sees the ocean — a force that cannot be resisted, only endured. But as he looked at the creature in front of him — twitching, burning, exposed — the moon fell away. The force became a man. No. Less than a man.
A parasite.
Parasites die when you take away their host.

Marcus grabbed Vlad's wrist and removed the hand from his throat. The gesture was almost gentle. Almost pitying. Vlad lurched backward, snarling — a sound that came from somewhere deep in his chest, from the same frequency that had shattered the lights minutes ago, but thinner now, weaker, a radio signal losing power with distance.
The NYSE security team chose that moment to arrive.

Three of them, tactical jackets and earpieces, moving in the confused formation of people who had been told there was an incident but had no framework for the incident they were actually encountering. One of them shouted something — "On the ground, now!" — and Vlad turned toward the sound with the desperate compliance of an animal seeking any exit.
He grabbed the nearest guard by the front of his jacket and threw him.

Thirty feet. The man sailed across the trading floor and hit a workstation with a sound like a car crash. The other two froze — not from fear, but from the sheer impossibility of what they'd just witnessed. Human bodies don't travel that way. Physics doesn't work like that.
The second guard recovered first. Drew his taser. Fired.

The probes hit Vlad in the chest. Fifty thousand volts arced through a body that was already conducting more electricity than it could hold — a body saturated with the wrong kind of signal, the 0.7Hz frequency that was not breathing, that was not heartbeat, that was something else entirely.
Vlad screamed.

Not the way a man screams. Not the way anything with lungs screams. The sound came from below the vocal range, below the hearing range, and then climbed — up through the subsonic, through the bass, through the register where the walls began to shake and the remaining intact screens on the trading floor started to spider-web with interference. The 0.7Hz frequency, amplified by the taser's current, ripped through the building's electrical system like a virus through unprotected code.
Every light in the New York Stock Exchange shattered.

Not metaphorically. Every fluorescent tube, every LED panel, every overhead projector, every indicator light on every terminal — they burst. Glass rained down from forty-foot ceilings. The sound was enormous, a cathedral of breaking, a thousand chandeliers hit with a thousand hammers. The trading floor plunged into absolute darkness.
Then the emergency lights kicked in.

Red. Strobing. Rotating beacons mounted in the ceiling, autonomous, battery-backed, turning the NYSE into a submarine in distress. In the pulsing crimson light, Alexander Vlad was a nightmare made of broken geometry — half a face of exquisite beauty, half a face of ancient rot, his body moving in stuttered frames between the strobes, retreating across the trading floor with the lurching gait of something that had forgotten how to be human.

The smell hit Maya like a wall. Burning skin. Burning hair. Ozone from the fried electronics, sharp and electric. And beneath it all, something older — the dry, graveyard smell of the thing Vlad had been hiding, now floating free in the air like a secret finally told.

She was on the floor. She didn't remember falling. The cold marble pressed against her palms, her knees. Glass fragments glittered in the emergency light like scattered diamonds. Her ears rang from the scream — or from the silence that followed it, which was somehow louder. The taste of smoke coated the inside of her mouth, acrid and charred, the flavor of a world ending and beginning in the same breath.
She could run. The exit was thirty feet away. No one would stop her. No one would blame her.

Maya Reyes reached into her bag and pulled out the portable audio recorder she'd brought. The same Tascam DR-40X she'd used to record every episode of *Unfollowed*. Her fingers found the record button by muscle memory alone.
She pressed it.

"Alexander Vlad is fleeing the New York Stock Exchange." Her voice was steady. Steadier than she felt. The red emergency light made her look like a war correspondent, which, she supposed, she was. "His face has been exposed on national television. The evidence of his crimes — human harvesting, blood farming, centuries of predation — is now a matter of public record." She paused. Took a breath. The smoke burned her lungs. "This is Maya Reyes. This is my podcast. And this is the end."

Vlad reached the emergency exit on the east side of the trading floor. He was deteriorating — she could see it in the strobing light, the way his silhouette was changing, becoming less defined, the edges of him softening like a photograph left in water. The emergency lights overhead emitted trace UV — industrial safety standards required it for color rendering — and even that minimal exposure was continuing the work the flash panels had started. His skin was smoking. Not burning, not yet, but smoking, the way paper does before it catches.
He pushed through the door.
November daylight.

The sun hit Alexander Vlad at 2:47 PM Eastern Standard Time on November 15, 2026. Full, direct, unfiltered sunlight — the kind of light that makes New Yorkers squint and pull their coats tighter, the kind of light that is absolutely indifferent to the creatures that walk beneath it.
For the first time in five hundred years, Alexander Vlad stood in the sun.
His skin ignited.

Not slowly. Not the way the movies showed it — no gradual reddening, no careful burning, no time for a final speech or a dramatic gesture. Instant. Blue-white flames erupted across his body with a sound like gas catching, like a furnace igniting, and the heat that rolled back through the emergency door was physical, palpable, a wall of thermal energy that pushed Maya's hair back from her face even from thirty feet away.
Vlad did not scream.

He was beyond screaming. The thing that had made the 0.7Hz frequency, the thing that had mimicked breath for five centuries — it was gone before the sound could form. The blue-white flames consumed what was left of the mask first: the cheekbones, the jaw, the last remnant of the face that had charmed billions. Beneath it, the ancient corpse burned with a fierce, chemical intensity — five hundred years of accumulated biomatter, stolen blood, preserved tissue, all of it releasing at once in a conflagration that was over almost before it began.

The cameras outside — the news crews, the smartphone holders, the security systems, the satellite uplinks — captured everything. Every frame. Every angle. The burning figure in the doorway of the New York Stock Exchange, backlit by the November sun, wreathed in blue-white fire. A funeral pyre on Wall Street. It played on every screen in the world, live, unedited, undeniable.
The flames died.

Where Vlad had stood, there was only ash. Fine, gray, scattering in the November wind across Wall Street. It rose and curled and drifted, mixing with the ordinary dust of New York City, joining the particulate matter of ten million lives. Some of it settled on the marble steps of the exchange. Some of it caught an updraft and vanished into the sky. Some of it landed on the lenses of the cameras and was wiped away.
Maya stood inside the NYSE, her recorder still running. The red light on the Tascam blinked steadily — recording silence, recording ash, recording history.
Marcus stood beside her.

He was staring at the place where Vlad had been. His face was unreadable — not blank, not empty, but so full of something that it couldn't resolve into any single expression. Five hundred years of fear. Five hundred years of rage. Five hundred years of grief for a self he couldn't remember losing. All of it pressing against the inside of his skin, looking for a way out.
He opened his mouth.

A single word. Not "Marcus." Something older. Something from a language that hadn't been spoken in four centuries, from a village that had been submerged to make way for a dam, from a life that had been stolen one cricket at a time.
"Willehm."
His real name.

The name he couldn't remember for five hundred years. It came back to him now, arriving with the ash-scented breeze that drifted through the open door — returned to him, perhaps, by the same force that had taken Vlad away. As if the universe maintained a balance. As if every parasite that dies returns something to the host.

He looked at Maya. His eyes were wet but clear — not the rheumy, conditioned tears of five centuries of submission, but something clean and new. Human tears. The kind that come when you remember who you are.
"Thank you."
He walked toward the door.
Toward the sunlight.

The same sunlight that had consumed Vlad in seconds. The same ultraviolet radiation, the same lethal dose of electromagnetic radiation that should have been fatal to anything that had shared a vampire's table for five hundred years. Maya's hand moved toward him — don't, she almost said, don't go out there — but the word died in her throat.
Marcus stepped through the door.
The November sun touched his skin.
He didn't burn.

The light hit his face, his hands, the shoulders of his coat. It warmed him — not with the blue-white violence of combustion but with the simple, ordinary warmth of a late autumn afternoon. The kind of warmth that says: you are alive. You are here. You are real.

Marcus Webb stood in the sunlight on Wall Street, and the ash of his former master drifted around him like snow, and he was not the monster. He had never been the monster. He had been a hostage, and the hostage was free.
He tilted his face up. Closed his eyes. The sun was warm on his eyelids — red and gold, the color of beginning.
For the first time since 1523, Willehm felt the sun.
And it was good.
